event: The Lamentation over the Dead Christ
symbols: Dead body of Christ — the sacrificial Lamb whose death atones for sin
Mary cradling Christ's head — Mater Dolorosa, the sword of Simeon's prophecy piercing her soul
Magdalene at Christ's side — faithful devotion to the end, the forgiven sinner clinging to her Redeemer
Angels above — heavenly mourning over the death of the Incarnate God
Anointing jar — preparation for burial, echoing the Magdalene's earlier anointing
description: The dead Christ lies in the foreground, his pale body stretched across the laps of mourners. The Virgin Mary in a white head covering supports his head, her face drawn with grief. A woman with golden hair kneels at his side. Behind them, bearded men look on in sorrow. Above the scene, an angel with outstretched arms descends dramatically from the sky. A vessel sits on the ground nearby. The composition is tall and vertical, framed by an arched top, with the angel's dynamic pose contrasting the stillness of the dead Christ below.
theological_description: The Lamentation is the moment between the Crucifixion and the Entombment when the full cost of redemption is laid bare in grief. Mary holds the body of her Son, fulfilling Simeon's prophecy: 'A sword will pierce through your own soul also' (LUK.2.35). The Magdalene's presence recalls her devotion throughout Christ's ministry — she who was forgiven much loved much (LUK.7.47). The angel descending from above underscores that this death, though fully human in its grief, is cosmically significant — the Creator has died for His creation. Isaiah's prophecy is visually fulfilled: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief' (ISA.53.3). The Fathers read the Lamentation through the lens of Paschal theology: this apparent defeat is the prelude to victory, this death the seed of resurrection. The body will be laid in the tomb, but on the third day it will rise.
